text.title: Data Quality Rules and Business Rules
text.summary: Six quality dimensions with measurable metrics and data business rule types with examples.

rule.statement: Assign each data quality dimension a numeric score (1-5), a traffic-light status (Green/Yellow/Red), and a remediation priority (H/M/L) in the assessment report.
rule.rationale: Qualitative assessments ("data is inconsistent") produce no remediation priority; numeric scores enable comparison across dimensions and tracking over time.

rule.statement: Never declare a dimension "Green" without a measured sample from production data of at least 100 records or 10% of total volume, whichever is larger.
rule.rationale: Small samples miss systemic issues; a 1% error rate on 10,000 records represents 100 corrupted records that will affect operations.

rule.statement: Document every business rule as one of: Validation (field-level constraint), Derivation (formula), Default (initial value), Constraint (cross-field or cross-entity), or Uniqueness (key definition).
rule.rationale: Mixing rule types in a single list makes it impossible to generate validators, ETL transformations, and database constraints from the same document.

list.item: Validation: Email must contain @ and a domain with at least one dot
Derivation: OrderTotal = sum(LineItem.Quantity * LineItem.Price) + ShippingCost - DiscountAmount
Default: CustomerStatus defaults to "Prospect" on record creation
Constraint: ShipDate must be on or after OrderDate
Uniqueness: CustomerID is system-generated and immutable; Email is unique across all active customers

antipattern.description: No data dictionary — every developer defines data elements independently.
antipattern.reason: Without shared definitions, integration and reporting produce conflicting numbers that erode trust in all data.

antipattern.description: Skipping data quality assessment — assuming existing data is correct before migration.
antipattern.reason: Data quality issues discovered during migration cause project delays; assessing early allows remediation to run in parallel with development.

antipattern.description: No data ownership defined — no one accountable for data accuracy or access.
antipattern.reason: Unowned data degrades over time; ownership assigns the accountability needed to maintain quality rules.

antipattern.description: Ignoring historical data — modeling current state without considering how data changes over time.
antipattern.reason: Temporal requirements (audit trails, soft deletes, effective-date ranges) discovered after the physical model is built require schema rework.

text.reference: Quality assessment report with per-dimension scoring
